architecture.summary: Map-tuning is a plug-and-play knowledge injection method that trains a lightweight affine mapping network to transform entity embeddings (from TransE or similar knowledge representation models) into the token embedding space of a frozen pre-trained language model. Entity mentions in input text are identified, their embeddings are mapped and projected, and the mapped representations replace or augment the corresponding token embeddings, allowing knowledge from external bases (Wikidata, UMLS) to flow into downstream tasks without retraining the model backbone.
trained_components: mapping network (affine transformation from entity embedding space to token embedding space)
Mention-Masked Language Modeling objective (for general map-tuning)

biblio.title: Plug-and-Play Knowledge Injection for Pre-trained Language Models

claims.statement: Map-tuning achieves consistent improvement on almost all downstream models in general plug-and-play injection, while existing methods (E-BERT, PELT, RA, K-Adapter) cannot consistently improve performance and are not suitable for the plug-and-play paradigm
On FewRel 1.0 (5-1 shot) with fine-tuning, map-tuning achieves 92.6 accuracy compared to 91.0 for baseline
On Wiki80 (10-1 shot) with fine-tuning, map-tuning achieves 88.1 accuracy compared to 85.4 for baseline
Task-specific map-tuning on Wiki80 achieves 87.8 accuracy when initialized from general map-tuning, compared to 86.1 for fine-tuning baseline
General map-tuning training on NVIDIA Tesla V100 costs only 2 hours, compared to 3000+ GPU hours for knowledge-enhanced pre-training methods like LUKE and KEPLER
Map-tuning optimizes only 0.1% of parameters of the downstream model
In domain adaptation from FewRel 1.0 (Wikipedia) to FewRel 2.0 (PubMed) at 5-1 shot, task-specific map-tuning achieves 81.2 accuracy compared to 76.7 for source domain baseline

Map-tuning was not evaluated on PLMs pre-trained with language modeling objectives other than MLM, and did not evaluate whether the PLM can perform complex reasoning (e.g., multi-hop reasoning) based on injected knowledge
